patterns.name: Singular and Plural Mix-Up
patterns.what: Sometimes confuses whether a subject is singular or plural, which leads to picking the wrong verb form.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Andrea picked the plural verb 'were' instead of the singular 'was' for the main subject 'Jane'.
She wrote the singular verb 'has' instead of 'have' for the plural subject 'hawker centres'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a blank for a verb, always ask yourself: **'Who or what is doing this action?'**
- Trace it back to the main subject and check if it is one (singular) or more than one (plural).
- Ignore extra information like **'together with'** or **'as well as'** that tries to trick you into thinking the subject is plural!

patterns.name: Spelling by Sound
patterns.what: Occasionally spells tricky words exactly how they sound, which leads to missing silent letters or using the wrong vowels.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Andrea spelled 'extinct' as 'extint', missing the silent 'c'.
She spelled 'inspiration' as 'insperation', using an 'e' instead of an 'i'.
She spelled 'sense' as 'sence', mixing up the 's' and 'c' sounds.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When checking your work, look out for words with tricky vowel sounds or silent letters.
- Break longer words into smaller chunks (syllables) to make sure no letters are left out.
- If a word looks a bit strange after you write it, try writing it another way on rough paper to see which one **looks right**.

patterns.name: Vague Story Answers
patterns.what: Tends to give general answers to comprehension questions instead of pointing to the specific event or action from the passage.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Andrea answered that the teacher 'thinks it is important' instead of giving the specific reason that Ah Huat had no time at home.
She referred generally to 'the boy's act' instead of stating exactly what the act was (getting stabbed by a pencil).
She wrote that the author 'learnt that he should not have done what he had done' instead of explaining that he experienced the same pain.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see prompts asking **'what made'** or **'explain why'**, always look for the exact action or event in the text.
- Avoid using vague words like **'it'**, **'the act'**, or **'what he did'**. Name the exact thing!
- Ask yourself: **'If someone hasn't read the story, would they understand my answer?'** If not, add more specific details.

patterns.name: Word Form Mix-Up
patterns.what: Sometimes uses a verb when the sentence needs a noun, or an adjective when it needs a noun.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Andrea wrote the verb 'is' instead of a noun like 'significance' or 'value' to describe the cultural importance of hawker centres.
She wrote the verb 'relieve' instead of the noun 'relief' in the phrase 'Much to Mr Fandi's relief'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
Look at the words right before the blank to figure out what type of word is needed.
- If you see words like **'the'**, **'a'**, or an apostrophe **'s'** (like **'Mr Fandi's'**), you almost always need a **noun** (a naming word).
- Read the whole sentence softly to yourself to hear if the word form fits smoothly.
